name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَطَمَسْنَا عَلَىٰٓ أَعْيُنِهِمْ فَٱسْتَبَقُوا۟ ٱلصِّرَٰطَ فَأَنَّىٰ يُبْصِرُونَ (٦٦)
```

```english
Had We wished We would have blotted out their eyes: then, were they to advance towards the path, how would have they seen? (66)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَمَسَخْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِهِمْ فَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ مُضِيًّا وَلَا يَرْجِعُونَ (٦٧)
```

```english
And had We wished We would have deformed them in their place; then they would neither have been able to move ahead nor to return. (67)
```

```arabic
وَمَن نُّعَمِّرْهُ نُنَكِّسْهُ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ ۖ أَفَلَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٦٨)
```

```english
And whomever We give a long life, We cause him to regress in creation. Then, will they not exercise their reason? (68)
```

```arabic
وَمَا عَلَّمْنَـٰهُ ٱلشِّعْرَ وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَقُرْءَانٌ مُّبِينٌ (٦٩)
```

```english
We did not teach him poetry, nor does it behoove him. This is just a reminder and a manifest Quran, (69)
```

```arabic
لِّيُنذِرَ مَن كَانَ حَيًّا وَيَحِقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧٠)
```

```english
so that anyone who is alive may be warned, and that the word may come due against the faithless. (70)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَا لَهُم مِّمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينَآ أَنْعَـٰمًا فَهُمْ لَهَا مَـٰلِكُونَ (٧١)
```

```english
Have they not seen that We have created for them—of what Our hands have worked—cattle, so they have become their masters? (71)
```

```arabic
وَذَلَّلْنَـٰهَا لَهُمْ فَمِنْهَا رَكُوبُهُمْ وَمِنْهَا يَأْكُلُونَ (٧٢)
```

```english
And We made them tractable for them; so some of them make their mounts and some of them they eat. (72)
```

```arabic
وَلَهُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ وَمَشَارِبُ ۖ أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٧٣)
```

```english
There are other benefits for them therein, and drinks. Will they not then give thanks? (73)
```

```arabic
وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً لَّعَلَّهُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٧٤)
```

```english
They have taken gods besides Allah, [hoping] that they might be helped [by the fake deities]. (74)
```

```arabic
لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُندٌ مُّحْضَرُونَ (٧٥)
```

```english
[But] they cannot help them, while they [themselves] are an army mobilized for their defence. (75)
```

```arabic
فَلَا يَحْزُنكَ قَوْلُهُمْ ۘ إِنَّا نَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٧٦)
```

```english
So do not let their remarks grieve you. We indeed know whatever they hide and whatever they disclose. (76)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَرَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ فَإِذَا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧٧)
```

```english
Does not man see that We created him from a drop of [seminal] fluid, and, behold, he is an open contender!? (77)
```

```arabic
وَضَرَبَ لَنَا مَثَلًا وَنَسِىَ خَلْقَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ مَن يُحْىِ ٱلْعِظَـٰمَ وَهِىَ رَمِيمٌ (٧٨)
```

```english
He draws comparisons for Us, and forgets his own creation. He says, ‘Who shall revive the bones when they have decayed?’ (78)
```

```arabic
قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَهَآ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ (٧٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘He will revive them who produced them the first time, and He has knowledge of all creation. (79)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّجَرِ ٱلْأَخْضَرِ نَارًا فَإِذَآ أَنتُم مِّنْهُ تُوقِدُونَ (٨٠)
```

```english
He, who made for you fire out of the green tree, and, behold, you light fire from it! (80)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَيْسَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُم ۚ بَلَىٰ وَهُوَ ٱلْخَلَّـٰقُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٨١)
```

```english
Is not He who created the heavens and the earth able to create the like of them? Yes indeed! He is the All-creator, the All-knowing. (81)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَآ أَمْرُهُۥٓ إِذَآ أَرَادَ شَيْـًٔا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٨٢)
```

```english
All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. (82)
```

```arabic
فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى بِيَدِهِۦ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٨٣)
```

```english
So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things, and to whom you shall be brought back. (83)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a summary exposition of the preceding meanings in a different context. It contains a threat of punishment for them, an indication that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is a Messenger and that his Book is a Reminder (الذكر) and a Qur’an—not the work of a poet nor is his Book poetry—an allusion to the creation of cattle as a sign (آية) of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and an argumentation for the Resurrection (الميعاد).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَطَمَسْنا عَلى أَعْيُنِهِمْ فَاسْتَبَقُوا الصِّراطَ فَأَنَّى يُبْصِرُونَ** (Had We wished, We would have surely effaced their eyes, and they would have raced for the path. Then how could they have seen? 36:66). It is stated in *al-Majma‘ al-Bayan*: "**Effacement (الطمس)** is the erasing of a thing until its trace disappears. Thus, effacing the eye is like effacing a book, and similarly, the effacing of wealth is its removal such that it can no longer be perceived. A blind person is called **effaced (مطموس)** or **obliterated (طميس)** when the slit between the eyelids disappears." End quote.

Thus, His saying: **وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَطَمَسْنا عَلى أَعْيُنِهِمْ** means: Had We willed, We would have removed their eyes so they would become smooth with no trace left, thereby taking away their sight and nullifying their vision.

His saying: **فَاسْتَبَقُوا الصِّراطَ** means: They would have intended to race toward the clear path (الصراط) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — which one who seeks it does not miss and one who travels it does not stray from, yet they would not have seen it and would never see it. The sense of impossibility understood from His saying: **فَأَنَّى يُبْصِرُونَ** (Then how could they have seen?) is a metaphor (كناية) for total prevention.

The view of some that "racing for the path" refers to their rushing to follow the path of truth and their failure to be guided to it is not without some far-fetchedness (بعد).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَمَسَخْناهُمْ عَلى مَكانَتِهِمْ فَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا مُضِيًّا وَلا يَرْجِعُونَ** (And had We wished, We would have surely transformed them in their places; then they would be able neither to move ahead nor to return. 36:67). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "**Metamorphosis (المسخ)** is the turning of a form into a disfigured creation, just as a people were transformed into apes and swine." He also said: "**Place (المكانة)** and **location (المكان)** are the same." End quote. The meaning of transforming them "in their places" is the disfigurement of their creation while they are sitting in the very spot they occupy, without changing them from their state through any treatment or exertion, but by mere Will (المشية). This is a metaphor for it being effortless and easy for Him, the Exalted, without any difficulty.

His saying: **فَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا مُضِيًّا وَلا يَرْجِعُونَ** means: They could not move forward (مضياً) into the punishment, nor could they return to their state prior to the punishment and metamorphosis. Thus, "moving forward" and "returning" are metaphors for returning to a state of well-being or remaining in a state of punishment and metamorphosis.

It has been said that it refers to their moving toward their objectives and returning to their homes and families, but this is not without some far-fetchedness.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ نُعَمِّرْهُ نُنَكِّسْهُ فِي الْخَلْقِ أَفَلا يَعْقِلُونَ** (Whomever We give a long life, We cause him to regress in creation. Do they not exercise their reason? 36:68). **Granting long life (التعمير)** is the lengthening of one's lifespan, and **regression (التنكيس)** is the overturning of a thing such that its top becomes its bottom, and its strength turns into weakness and its increase into decrease. A human being in the stage of senility is regressed in creation; his strength turns to weakness, his knowledge to ignorance, and his memory to forgetfulness.

The verse is in the position of citing the regression of creation as evidence for the possibility of the content of the two preceding verses. The meaning is that He who regresses the creation of a human being when He grants him long life is capable of effacing their eyes and transforming them in their places.

In His saying: **أَفَلا يَعْقِلُونَ** (Do they not exercise their reason?), there is a rebuke for their lack of intellection and an urging for them to contemplate these matters and take heed from them.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَقُرْآنٌ مُبِينٌ** (We did not teach him poetry, nor does it behoove him. This is just a reminder and a manifest Qur’an, 36:69). This is a conjunction and a return to what was mentioned at the beginning of the Surah regarding the confirmation of the Messenger's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) message and that his Book is a revelation (تنزيل) from Him, the Exalted.

Thus, His saying: **وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ** (We did not teach him poetry) negates that He taught him poetry. The necessary implication is that he is such that he does not compose poetry well—not that he is capable of it but refrains from it due to a prohibition from Allah directed at him, nor that the revealed Qur’an is not poetry even if he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were able to compose it.

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: **وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُ** (nor does it behoove him) is in the position of bestowing a favor upon him by purifying him from composing poetry. The sentence serves to repel a potential objection. The gist is that Our not teaching him poetry does not entail a deficiency in him, nor is it an incapacitation; rather, it is to elevate his rank and to sanctify his presence from that which besets one who is skilled in the craft of poetry—falling into the trap of decorating meanings with false poetic imaginings (التخيلات الشعرية الكاذبة) which, the more they are delved into, the more the speech affects the soul, and organizing speech with musical meters to make it more pleasing to the ear. Thus, it does not behoove him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to compose poetry while he is a Messenger from Allah, and the sign of his message and the core of his call is the Qur’an, which is miraculous in its eloquence—a Reminder (الذكر) and a manifest Qur’an.

His saying: **إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَقُرْآنٌ مُبِينٌ** (This is just a reminder and a manifest Qur’an) is an explanation and clarification of His saying: **وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُ**, since its necessary meaning is that the Qur’an is not poetry. The restriction (الحصر) understood from His saying: **إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ** etc., is a "restriction of reversal" (قصر القلب); the meaning is: It is not poetry; it is nothing but a Reminder and a manifest Qur’an.

The meaning of it being a "Reminder" and a "Qur’an" is that it is a Reminder recited from Allah; this is its apparent sense.

His saying, the Exalted: **لِيُنْذِرَ مَنْ كانَ حَيًّا وَيَحِقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ** (so that he may warn him who is alive, and that the word may come true against the faithless. 36:70). This is a causal clause (تعليل) connected to His saying: **وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ**. The meaning is: We did not teach him poetry so that he may warn, by means of the Qur’an—which is purified from being poetry—him who is alive, etc. Or it is connected to His saying: **إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ** etc., meaning: That which he recites to the people is nothing but a Reminder and a manifest Qur’an which We sent down to him so that he may warn him who is alive, etc. The outcome of both views is the same.

The verse—as you see—considers the ultimate purpose of sending the Messenger and revealing the Qur’an to be the warning of him who is alive—which is a metaphor for one who intellectually perceives the truth and hears it—and the realization and necessity of the Word against the disbelievers (الكافرين) — *those who reject the truth after it has become manifest*. The correspondence of this verse to the verses at the beginning of the Surah regarding this meaning is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ مِمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينا أَنْعاماً فَهُمْ لَها مالِكُونَ** (Have they not seen that We have created for them cattle from what Our hands have wrought, and they are their masters? 36:71). This mentions one of the signs of monotheism (التوحيد) that points to His Lordship (الربوبية) and His governance (التدبير) of the human world. It is similar to what preceded among the earlier signs of monotheism, such as the reviving of the dead earth by bringing forth grain and fruits and the gushing of springs.

The meaning of the cattle being "from what Our hands have wrought" is the absence of any partner in their creation and its exclusivity to Him, the Exalted. Thus, the "work of the hands" is a metaphor for exclusivity (الاختصاص).

His saying: **فَهُمْ لَها مالِكُونَ** (and they are their masters) is a derivation from His saying: **خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ** (We have created for them), for the meaning is: We created them for their sake. Thus, they are created for the sake of the human being, and the necessary consequence is their exclusivity to him. This exclusivity culminates in ownership (الملك), for the conventional ownership (الملك الاعتباري) found in society is a branch of exclusivity.

By this, the weakness in the statement of some becomes clear, who said that the derivation of **فَهُمْ لَها مالِكُونَ** from **خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ** is obscure, and that it is apparently derived from an omitted phrase, the estimation being: "We created them for them, so they are their owners." You are well aware that its derivation from **خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ** is not obscure and there is no need for such an estimation.

It has also been said that "ownership" (الملك) here means power and subjugation, but this is refuted by the fact that this is understood from the subsequent verse: **وَذَلَّلْناها لَهُمْ** (And We have subdued them for them), and establishing a new meaning (التأسيس) is better than mere emphasis (التأكيد).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَذَلَّلْناها لَهُمْ فَمِنْها رَكُوبُهُمْ وَمِنْها يَأْكُلُونَ** (And We have subdued them for them, so some of them they ride and some of them they eat. 36:72). Subduing the cattle means making them compliant and non-rebellious, which is their subjugation (تسخير) for them. **The mount (الركوب)**—with a *fatha* on the *ra*—refers to beasts of burden like camels and oxen. His saying: **وَمِنْها يَأْكُلُونَ** means they eat from their meat.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَهُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ وَمَشارِبُ أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ** (And in them they have [other] benefits and drinks. Will they not then give thanks? 36:73). The "benefits" (المنافع) refer to what they utilize of their hair, wool, skins, and so on. **Drinks (المشارب)** is the plural of *mashrab*—a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) used in the sense of the object—referring to milk. The discussion regarding the meaning of gratitude (الشكر) is the same as what preceded in His saying: **وَما عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ**.

The meaning of these three verses is: Have they not known that We created for their sake and for the governance of their worldly life cattle—camels, oxen, and sheep—and it followed from that that they are their owners with an ownership that validates all types of their disposal over them without any opponent? And We subdued them for them by making them subjugated and compliant, not rebellious; so from them is their mount which they ride, and from them—meaning from their meat—they eat. And they have in them benefits, utilizing their hair, wool, and skins, and drinks from their milk which they drink. Will they not then thank Allah for this perfect governance which reveals His Lordship over them? Or will they not worship Him in gratitude for His favors?

His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ آلِهَةً لَعَلَّهُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ** (They have taken gods besides Allah, [hoping] that they might be helped. 36:74). The plural pronouns refer to the polytheists. The "gods" (آلهة) refer to idols, or devils, or human tyrants (فراعنة البشر), excluding the near-stationed angels and the saintly humans, because the end of the passage—**وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُحْضَرُونَ**—does not suit them.

They only took them as gods in the hope that they might be helped by them, because the common folk among them take a god under the assumption that the management of their affairs—whether good or evil—is delegated to the one they have taken as a god. Thus, the worshiper among them worships it to please it with his worship, so that it does not become angry and cut off the blessing or send down a calamity.

His saying, the Exalted: **لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُحْضَرُونَ** (They cannot help them, yet they are an army [always] present for them. 36:75). Meaning: These gods they have taken cannot help these polytheists, for they possess nothing of good or evil.

As for His saying: **وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُحْضَرُونَ**, it is apparent that the first of the two pronouns refers to the polytheists and the second to the gods besides Allah. The meaning is that the polytheists are an army (جند) for the gods. This is because one of the requirements of the meaning of "soldiery" (الجندية) is following and accompaniment, and it is the polytheists who are counted as followers of their gods, obeying them, not the other way around.

The meaning of "being present" (الإحضار) in His saying: **مُحْضَرُونَ** is being brought for recompense on the Day of Resurrection. The Exalted said: **وَجَعَلُوا بَيْنَهُ وَبَيْنَ الْجِنَّةِ نَسَباً وَلَقَدْ عَلِمَتِ الْجِنَّةُ إِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ** (They have set up a kinship between Him and the jinn, while the jinn certainly know that they will be [summoned and] presented [before Him]. 37:158) and He said: **وَلَوْ لا نِعْمَةُ رَبِّي لَكُنْتُ مِنَ الْمُحْضَرِينَ** (Had it not been for my Lord’s blessing, I would have surely been among those [summoned and] presented [in hell]. 37:57). The gist of the meaning is: The adopted gods cannot help the polytheists, while they—the polytheists—are for them—the gods—obedient followers, brought together with them on the Day of Resurrection.

As for the view of those who say the meaning is that the polytheists are an army for their gods prepared to defend them in this world, or that the meaning is that they—the gods—are for them—the polytheists—an army brought for the punishment of the polytheists on the Day of Resurrection because they are the fuel of the Fire with which the polytheists are punished, or brought for their punishment to demonstrate their inability to help or to make the polytheists despair of their intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — these are poor interpretations.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلا يَحْزُنْكَ قَوْلُهُمْ إِنَّا نَعْلَمُ ما يُسِرُّونَ وَما يُعْلِنُونَ** (So let not their speech grieve you. Indeed We know what they hide and what they disclose. 36:76). The *fa* is for deriving the prohibition of grief from the reality of their taking gods besides Allah in hope of help. That is: If this is the reality of their state—that those from whom they sought help cannot help them at all and that they will be brought with them for punishment—then let not their speech regarding polytheism (الشرك) grieve you. For We are not heedless of them such that they could frustrate Us or spoil any matter for Us; rather, We know what they hide of their speech and what they disclose. In the syntax of the verse, there are some poor views which we have ignored.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَ الْإِنْسانُ أَنَّا خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ فَإِذا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُبِينٌ** (Does not man see that We created him from a drop? Then behold, he is an open adversary! 36:77). This is a return to the subject of the Resurrection (البعث) and the argumentation for it following their denial. It is not unlikely that it is a detailed explanation of their "speech" referred to in His saying: **فَلا يَحْزُنْكَ قَوْلُهُمْ** etc. The "seeing" (الرؤية) here refers to certain knowledge (العلم القطعي). That is: Does not man know with certain knowledge that We created him from a drop (نطفة)? The indefiniteness of "drop" is for belittlement. The "adversary" (الخصيم) is one who persists in his adversity and argumentation.

The interrogative is for wonder (التعجب). The meaning is: It is wondrous that man knows We created him from a lowly drop, yet he suddenly becomes an open, argumentative adversary.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَضَرَبَ لَنا مَثَلاً وَنَسِيَ خَلْقَهُ قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ** (He draws an example for Us, and forgets his own creation. He says, ‘Who shall revive the bones when they have decayed?’ 36:78). **Decayed (الرميم)** refers to worn-out bones. **وَنَسِيَ خَلْقَهُ** (and forgets his own creation) is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the subject of "draws." His saying: **قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ** is an explanation of the example the man drew. For this reason, it is brought as a separate sentence without a conjunction, because the speech is in the sense of asking: "What example did he draw?" So it was said: "He said, 'Who shall revive the bones when they have decayed?'"

The meaning is: Man drew an example for Us while having forgotten his own creation from a drop the first time. Had he remembered it, he would not have drawn the example he drew, which is his saying: "Who shall revive the bones when they are worn out?" For he would have refuted himself and answered the example he drew by his first creation, just as Allah taught His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to answer him.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ** (Say, ‘He will revive them who produced them the first time, and He has knowledge of all creation. 36:79). This is the teaching of the answer to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

**Production (الإنشاء)** is the initial bringing into existence, and its qualification with His saying **أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** (the first time) is for emphasis. His saying: **وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ** (and He has knowledge of all creation) is an indication that He, the Exalted, does not forget nor is He ignorant of anything of His creation. Since He is the Creator of these bones the first time and He is not ignorant of anything they were before death or after it, then His reviving them a second time is entirely possible due to the presence of Power and the absence of ignorance and forgetfulness.

His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الشَّجَرِ الْأَخْضَرِ ناراً فَإِذا أَنْتُمْ مِنْهُ تُوقِدُونَ** (He who made for you fire out of the green tree, and behold, you light [your fires] from it. 36:80). This is an explanation of His saying: **الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ**. **Kindling (الإيقاد)** is the lighting of fire.

The verse is intended to remove the sense of impossibility of making a dead thing into something possessing life, while life and death are mutually exclusive. The answer is that there is no impossibility in it, for He is the one who made for you fire from the green tree that drips with water, and behold, you kindle and light fire from it. What is meant by it, according to the famous view among the exegetes, are the *Markh* and *‘Afar* trees. They used to take from them while they were green, making the *‘Afar* the lower fire-stick and the *Markh* the upper fire-stick; the upper would be rubbed against the lower, and fire would ignite by the permission of Allah. Thus, the emergence of the living from the dead is no more wondrous than the ignition of fire from a green tree, while they are opposites.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَوَلَيْسَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِقادِرٍ عَلى أَنْ يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُمْ بَلى وَهُوَ الْخَلَّاقُ الْعَلِيمُ** (Is not He who created the heavens and the earth able to create the like of them? Yes [indeed], and He is the All-creator, the All-knowing. 36:81). The interrogative is for negation (الإنكار). The verse is an explanation of the previous argument mentioned in His saying: **قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** etc., with an explanation closer to the mind. This is done by replacing their initial production with the creation of the heavens and the earth, which is greater than the creation of man, as the Exalted said: **لَخَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ** (The creation of the heavens and the earth is certainly greater than the creation of mankind. 40:57).

Thus, the verse is in the sense of our saying: How can it be said that Allah—who created the worlds of the heavens and the earth with all their vastness of creation, wondrous general system containing countless particular systems that dazzle the intellects and bewilder the minds, of which the human world is but a small part—is not able to create the like of these people? Yes, and He is the All-Creator (الخلاق), the All-Knowing (العليم).

Regarding the meaning of "the like of them" (مثلهم), it has been said: they and those like them. However, this is contrary to the meaning of "like" (مثل) as known in language and custom.

It has also been said: what is meant by "the like of them" is they themselves, by way of metaphor (كناية), similar to the saying: "The like of you is above such a thing," meaning "You are above it." However, if it were a metaphor, it would be correct to state it explicitly, but there would be no point in our saying: "Is not He who created the heavens and the earth able to create them?" for the discussion is about their resurrection, not their creation, and the polytheists admit that their Creator is Allah, the Glorified.

It has also been said: the pronoun in **مِثْلَهُمْ** refers to the heavens and the earth, as they include the rational beings within them, so the pronoun of rational beings was returned to them by way of predominance (تغليباً). Thus, the meaning is that Allah, the Creator of the world, is able to create its like.

However, the context is one of proving the resurrection of the human being, not the resurrection of the heavens and the earth. Furthermore, the discussion is about restoration (الإعادة), and creating the like of a thing is not the restoration of its very self (عينه), but necessarily something else.

The truth is to say: What is meant by "creating the like of them" is their restoration for recompense after death, as is understood from the words of al-Tabarsi (may Allah have mercy on him) in *al-Majma‘ al-Bayan*.

Its explanation is that the human being is composed of a soul (نفس) and a body (بدن). The body in this world is constantly in a state of dissolution and replacement; its parts are perpetually changing. A composite entity ceases to exist with the cessation of one of its parts; thus, in every moment, it is other than what it was in the previous moment in its physical person. Yet, the identity (شخصية) of the human being is preserved by his soul—his spirit (روحه)—which is immaterial (المجردة), purified from matter and the changes occurring from its side, and safe from death and corruption.

What is gathered from His speech, the Exalted, is that the soul does not die with the death of the body and that it is preserved until it returns to Allah, the Glorified, as was previously understood from His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ** (They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we be in a new creation?’ Indeed, they deny the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you fully, then you will be brought back to your Lord.’ 32:10-11).

Thus, the subsequent body of the human being, when considered in relation to his previous body, is its "like" (مثل), not its "self" (عين). However, the human being with the subsequent body, when measured against the human being with the previous body, is his "self," not his "like," because the identity is by the soul, and it is one and the same.

Since the polytheists' sense of impossibility in their saying: "Who shall revive the bones when they have decayed?" referred to the creation of the new body, not the soul, He, the Glorified, answered by proving the possibility of creating "the like of them." As for their return as their very selves, it is only completed by the attachment of the souls and spirits—preserved with Allah—to the newly created bodies. Thus, the persons existing in the world would be the people themselves, as the Exalted said: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللهَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَلَمْ يَعْيَ بِخَلْقِهِنَّ بِقادِرٍ عَلى أَنْ يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتى** (Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and was not exhausted by their creation, is able to revive the dead? 46:33). He linked the revival to the dead themselves, saying: (**عَلى أَنْ يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتى**) and He did not say: "to revive the like of the dead."

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** (All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. 36:82). This verse is among the most brilliant of the Qur’anic verses; it describes the word of bringing into existence (كلمة الإيجاد) and explains that He, the Exalted, in bringing into existence anything He has willed, does not need anything beyond His transcendent Essence—neither a cause to produce what He willed, nor an aid in its production, nor a repeller of any obstacle that might prevent Him.

His expression of this reality has varied in His speech; He said: **إِنَّما قَوْلُنا لِشَيْءٍ إِذا أَرَدْناهُ أَنْ نَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** (All Our word to a thing, when We will it, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is. 16:40), and He said: **وَإِذا قَضى أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** (When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. 2:117).

Regarding His saying: **إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ**, it is apparent that "command" (الأمر) here means "affair" or "state" (الشأن). Although His saying in the verse of *al-Nahl* quoted just now—**إِنَّما قَوْلُنا لِشَيْءٍ إِذا أَرَدْناهُ**—might support "command" being in the sense of "speech"—the verbal command with the word "Be" (كن)—nevertheless, contemplation of the verses shows that the purpose in them is to describe the Divine state (الشأن الإلهي) when willing the creation of something, not to state that His speech when creating something is this specific word and no other. Thus, it is better to interpret "speech" (القول) as "command" (الأمر) in the sense of "affair" (الشأن), meaning it was brought as an instance of the "affair," not to interpret "command" as "speech" in the sense of that which is the opposite of "prohibition."

His saying: **إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً** (when He wills something) means: when He wills the bringing into existence of something, as the context of the verse provides. In several verses, "decree" (القضاء) appears in place of "will" (الإرادة), such as His saying: **إِذا قَضى أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ**. There is no harm in this, for decree is the judgment (الحكم). Decree, judgment, and will from Allah are one thing, which is the being (كون) of the existing thing such that it has nothing from Allah, the Glorified, except that it exists. Thus, the meaning of "when We will it" is: when We place it in the position of the attachment of the Will.

His saying: **أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ** (is to say to it ‘Be’) is the predicate of **إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ**, meaning He addresses it with the word "Be." It is well known that there is no actual word being uttered, otherwise it would need another word for its own existence, and so on, leading to an infinite regress. Nor is there an addressee with hearing who hears the address and then exists by it, for that would lead to a contradiction. Rather, the speech is a representation (تمثيل) of His bestowing existence upon the thing without need for anything else beyond His transcendent Essence, and without failure or delay.

By this, the falsity of what some have mentioned becomes clear, where they said: "It is apparent that there is a verbal saying, which is the word 'Be,' and this was the view of most of the predecessors (السلف). The affairs of Allah, the Exalted, are beyond what intellects can reach, so leave aside talk and dispute." End quote.

This is because what he mentioned—that His affairs are beyond the reach of intellects—if it were to invalidate a definitive rational argument (الحجة العقلية القطعية), then the religious sciences would be invalidated from their very foundation. For the authenticity of the Book, for example, with the true knowledge it provides, is only established by rational argument. If the rational argument were invalidated by a book, a sunnah, or anything else that is itself established by it, then that invalidating evidence would be invalidating itself first. Thus, "let not a foot slip after it has been firm."

It is well known that there is nothing but Allah—exalted is His Name—and the thing that is brought into existence; there is no third between them. Attributing causality and agency to His Will apart from Him, the Exalted—while the Will is an active attribute (صفة فعلية) abstracted from the level of action, as previously mentioned—would entail the cessation of the things' need for Him, the Exalted, from the start, because it would necessitate the things' independence from Him through an attribute abstracted from them.

It is also well known that there is no "command" that separates from Him, the Exalted, called "bringing into existence" (إيجاد) and "existence" (وجود), which then attaches to the thing so that it becomes existent by it; this is obvious. Thus, there is nothing after Him, the Exalted, except the existence of the thing itself.

From here, it becomes clear that the word of bringing into existence—which is the word "Be" (كن)—is the very existence of the thing He has brought into existence, but insofar as it is attributed to Him and subsistent by Him. As for its attribution to itself, it is "existent" (موجود), not "bringing into existence" (إيجاد), and "created" (مخلوق), not "creation" (خلق).

It also becomes clear that what emanates from Him, the Exalted, accepts neither delay nor respite, nor does it tolerate change or transformation, nor is it clothed in gradualness (التدريج). What appears in creation of these matters only occurs in the things from the side of themselves, not from the side that faces their Lord, the Glorified. This is a door from which a thousand doors open.

In the verses, there are subtle indications alluding to these realities, such as His saying, the Exalted: **كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** (like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was. 3:59), and His saying: **وَما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ** (Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye. 54:50), and His saying: **وَكانَ أَمْرُ اللهِ قَدَراً مَقْدُوراً** (Allah’s command is a precise decree. 33:38), among others.

His saying at the end of the verse: **فَيَكُونُ** (and it is) is an explanation of the thing's obedience to Him, the Exalted, its compliance with the command "Be," and its donning of existence.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ** (So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things, and to whom you shall be brought back. 36:83). **Dominion (الملكوت)** is an intensive form of the meaning of "sovereignty" (الملك), like *rahmut* and *rahbut* for "mercy" and "fear."

The joining of this verse to the one before it indicates that "dominion" (الملكوت) refers to the side facing Him, the Exalted, of the two aspects of the existence of things, while "sovereignty" (الملك) refers to the side facing the creation, or the more general sense encompassing both aspects. Upon this is based His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** (Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude. 6:75), and His saying: **أَوَلَمْ يَنْظُرُوا فِي مَلَكُوتِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (Have they not contemplated the dominion of the heavens and the earth? 7:185), and His saying: **قُلْ مَنْ بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** (Say, ‘In whose hand is the dominion of all things?’ 23:88).

Placing the "dominion" in His hand, the Exalted, is to demonstrate that He is the absolute authority over it, and no one else has any share in it.

The outcome of the meaning of His saying: **فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** is His glorification from what they deemed impossible in their denial of the Resurrection, due to their heedlessness of the fact that the dominion of all things is in His hand and within His grasp.

His saying: **وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ** (and to whom you shall be brought back) is an address to all people—believers and polytheists—and an explanation of the result of the preceding exposition after the glorification.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُ**—the verse—he said: "Quraysh used to say: 'That which Muhammad says is poetry.' So Allah refuted them and said: **وَما عَلَّمْناهُ الشِّعْرَ وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَقُرْآنٌ مُبِينٌ**. And the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) never composed poetry."

In *al-Majma‘*, it is narrated from al-Hasan: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) once quoted this verse: 'Islam and gray hair are sufficient as a restrainer for a man.' Abu Bakr said to him: 'O Messenger of Allah, he only said: Gray hair and Islam are sufficient as a restrainer for a man. And I bear witness that you are the Messenger of Allah, and Allah did not teach you poetry, nor does it behoove you.'"

In the same source, from ‘A’ishah, she said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to quote a verse by the brother of Banu Qays:
*The days will reveal to you what you were ignorant of,*
*and one for whom you provided no sustenance will bring you news.*
He began saying: 'And one for whom you provided no sustenance will bring you news.' Abu Bakr would say: 'It is not like this, O Messenger of Allah.' He would reply: 'I am not a poet, nor does it behoove me.'"

I say: These two reports from al-Hasan and ‘A’ishah were also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, as he narrated them. Other instances of what he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) quoted were also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur*.

It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "As for his saying:
*I am the Prophet, no lie,*
*I am the son of ‘Abd al-Muttalib,*
some people have said: 'This is not poetry.' Others said: 'It was merely a coincidence on his part and he did not intend poetry.'" End quote. The couplet is narrated from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and they have discussed it extensively; dismissing the narration is easier than denying that it is poetry or intended poetry.

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لِيُنْذِرَ مَنْ كانَ حَيًّا**—the verse—it is said: "It is permissible that what is meant by 'him who is alive' is 'the rational one.' This has been narrated from ‘Ali (عليه السلام)."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام): regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ**—until His saying—**مُحْضَرُونَ**, he says: "The gods cannot help them, while they are an army present for the gods."

From the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, from al-Halabi from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), he said: "Ubayy ibn Khalaf came and took a decayed bone from a wall and crumbled it, then said: 'When we are bones and dust, shall we indeed be raised as a new creation?' So Allah revealed: **قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ**."

I say: The like of this was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through many chains from Ibn ‘Abbas and ‘Urwah ibn al-Zubayr—and from Qatadah, al-Suddi, and ‘Ikrimah. It was also narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas that the speaker was al-‘As ibn Wa’il—and through another chain from him—that the speaker was ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy.

In *al-Ihtijaj*: in the argumentation of Abu ‘Abdillah al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): The questioner said: "Does the soul vanish after leaving its mold, or does it remain?" He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Rather, it remains until the time the Trumpet is blown. At that point, things will be nullified and negated; there will be neither perceiver nor perceived. Then things will be restored as their Manager began them. That will be for four hundred years in which the creation will slumber—that is between the two blasts."

He said: "How can there be a resurrection when the body has decayed and the limbs have scattered—one limb in a town eaten by its predators, another in another torn by its vermin, and another turned to dust used with clay in a wall?"

He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "He who produced it from nothing and formed it without a prior example is capable of restoring it as He began it."

He said: "Clarify that for me." He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The soul is stationed in its place—the soul of the virtuous in light and spaciousness, and the soul of the wicked in constriction and darkness. The body becomes dust as it was created from it. Whatever the predators and vermin cast out from their bellies—what they ate and tore—all of that is preserved in the dust with One from whom not even the weight of an atom is hidden in the darknesses of the earth. He knows the number of things and their weight. Indeed, the dust of the spiritual ones is like gold in the dust.

When it is the time of Resurrection, the rain of the Resurrection will fall upon the earth. The earth will swell and then churn like the churning of a waterskin. The dust of humans will become like gold from dust when washed with water, and like butter from milk when churned. The dust of every mold will gather to its mold, and by the permission of the Powerful God, it will move to where the soul is. The forms will return by the permission of the Fashioner as they were, and the soul will enter them. Then, when he stands upright, he will not fail to recognize anything of himself."

In *Nahj al-Balaghah*: "He says when He intends its being: 'Be,' and it is—not with a sound that strikes, nor a call that is heard. Rather, His speech, the Glorified, is an act of His which He produced. Its like did not exist before that; had it been eternal, it would have been a second god."

In the same source: "He says but does not utter; He intends but does not harbor [thoughts]."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Safwan ibn Yahya, he said: "I said to Abu al-Hasan (عليه السلام): 'Inform me about the Will from Allah and from the creation.' He said: 'The will from the creation is the inner thought (الضمير) and what appears to them after that of action. As for Allah, His Will is His bringing into existence (إحداثه) and nothing else, because He does not deliberate, nor does He intend [in the human sense], nor does He reflect. These attributes are negated from Him; they are attributes of the creation.

Thus, the Will of Allah is the act and nothing else. He says to it: 'Be,' and it is, without a word, without speech with a tongue, without intention, without reflection, and without a 'how' for that, just as there is no 'how' for Him."

I say: The narrations from them (عليه السلام) regarding His Will being among the active attributes (صفات الفعل) are widely transmitted (مستفيضة).
